title: 11 Tahsas — Archangel Michael & The Assembly
narrative:
The eleventh day of Tahsas bears the monthly feast of the Archangel Michael, whose name in the Hebrew is a question and a proclamation simultaneously: 'Who is like God?' — a challenge hurled against the pride of Lucifer in the primordial warfare of heaven, and a proclamation of the absolute incomparability of the Most High. Michael is the captain of the angelic armies, the guardian of Israel, the conductor of souls to their divine judgment, and the protector of the Church in all her tribulations. In the Ethiopian liturgical tradition, Michael receives an extraordinary degree of devotion; his feast on the twelfth of each month is among the most solemn, and the churches dedicated to him throughout the highlands of Ethiopia are centers of pilgrimage and healing. The Archangel who cried 'Who is like God?' stands as the perfect witness to the mystery of Tahsas: for the Incarnation is precisely the divine answer to that question — God, who has no like, became like unto us.

Saint Daniel the Stylite, a fifth-century Syrian ascetic who spent thirty-three years of his life standing atop a pillar outside Constantinople, brings to this day the witness of a life elevated above the earthly in ceaseless contemplation. Daniel, whose name in the Hebrew means 'God is my judge,' was formed in the monastic tradition of Simeon Stylites and surpassed even his master in the duration of his elevated witness. From his column he directed the spiritual lives of thousands, received imperial visitors, withstood the assaults of heretics, and prayed without ceasing for the city spread beneath his feet; he descended from his pillar only once in all those years, at the command of the Patriarch, to rebuke a heretical emperor.

Saint Luke the Newer, a tenth-century Greek ascetic who is not to be confused with the Evangelist, adds a further note to this day's assembly. Luke was a young man of Thessaly who renounced a military career and became a wandering ascetic, practicing extreme poverty and fasting, and was eventually established in Corinth where his sanctity attracted disciples and his prayers brought healing to many. He departed this life peacefully and his body was found incorrupt, testifying to the transformation that divine grace works in human flesh — a transformation that is rooted in the mystery of the Incarnation, wherein the flesh of Adam is taken up by the divine Word and made capable of incorruption.

The theological seal of this eleventh day of Tahsas is the mystery of elevation. Michael is elevated above all created spirits as the captain of heaven; Daniel elevates his body upon a pillar as a living symbol of the soul raised above earthly passion; Luke elevates his life above the common round of human ambition by radical renunciation. All three speak of the paradox of the Incarnation, which is itself the supreme elevation in reverse: the Most High descends to the lowest point of creaturely existence, that He might raise all creation to the heights of divine participation.
